title: Quantum Geometric Origin of Hall Viscosity and Nonlocal Hall Conductivity in Lattice Bands
authors: Danyu Shu, Ryotaro Sano, Ai Yamakage, Hiroshi Funaki, Mamoru Matsuo
abstract: We show that Hall viscosity in lattice bands is governed by a band-projected electric quadrupole encoded within the quantum geometry: Berry curvature sets the projected-coordinate algebra, while the quantum metric determines the quadrupolar spread of a wave packet. The same structure enters the quadratic wave-vector coefficient of the nonlocal Hall conductivity, yielding a lattice viscosity-conductivity relation. In ideal bands, the deviation from the Landau-level form is quantified by Berry curvature fluctuations. Our results establish the nonlocal Hall response as an electrical signature of the quantum geometry underlying Hall viscosity and as a transport diagnostic of geometric idealness.
summary: This paper connects the quantum geometry of lattice bands to measurable transport properties like Hall viscosity and nonlocal Hall conductivity. It demonstrates that the spatial extent of a wave packet (the electric quadrupole) links these two phenomena, providing a way to use electrical measurements to diagnose how closely a lattice system mimics the ideal physics of Landau levels.
main_problem: The paper seeks to identify measurable electrical observables that capture the quantum geometry (Berry curvature and quantum metric) in lattice bands to serve as a proxy for Hall viscosity.
main_result: The authors establish that Hall viscosity in lattice bands is governed by a band-projected electric quadrupole and propose that the nonlocal Hall conductivity serves as a transport diagnostic for the 'geometric idealness' of a band.
method: The study uses a semiclassical wave packet approach, the Kubo formula for linear response, and a projected position operator framework to relate geometric tensors to transport coefficients.
model_or_system: The study focuses on isolated, gapped Bloch bands, specifically examining Chern bands, moiré flat bands (chiral twisted bilayer graphene), the Kapit-Mueller model, and the Qi-Wu-Zhang model.
key_observables: Hall viscosity, nonlocal Hall conductivity, Berry curvature, quantum metric, and the nonlocal Hall ratio (R).
important_parameters: Adiabatic regime, Berry curvature fluctuations, and the trace condition (tr[g] = |Omega|).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes an adiabatic regime where interband mixing is negligible and focuses on isolated single energy bands.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a conceptual schematic of the electric quadrupole underlying Hall responses; Figure 2 compares the nonlocal Hall ratio R across different models (KM, chiral TBG, QWZ) against theoretical predictions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of lattice Hall viscosity, develops an algebraic framework using projected position operators, establishes the viscosity-conductivity relation, and validates the results using specific lattice models (KM, QWZ, and chiral TBG).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Conceptual schematic of the geometric quadrupole picture. The quantum metric and Berry curvature define the band-projected electric quadrupole, which underlies (a) the Hall viscous response to dynamical strain and (b) the non- local Hall response to a spatially nonuniform electric field. Green and pink arrows in the lower panels indicate the ap- plied perturbations and induced Hall responses, respectively.

FIG. 2. Transport signature of geometric idealness. The non- local Hall ratio R is compared with the ideal-band expres- sion 1 + F 2 Ωfor three representative Chern bands: Kapit– Mueller (KM), chiral twisted bilayer graphene (chiral TBG), and Qi–Wu–Zhang (QWZ) models. The dashed line denotes the Landau-level (LL) benchmark R = 1, corresponding to the ideal-band limit with uniform Berry curvature.
